title: Share of the population in poverty vs. Electoral Democracy Index
subtitle: Poverty is defined as living on less than $10 a day. This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries. The electoral democracy index is by [V-Dem](#dod:v-dem), based on expert estimates, and ranges from 0 to 1 (most democratic).
caption: In 2025, the share of the population living on less than $10 a day varies widely across countries with different levels of electoral democracy. The scatter plot places the Electoral Democracy Index on the horizontal axis, ranging from 0 to 1, and the poverty rate on the vertical axis, ranging from 0% to over 90%. A negative relationship is visible: countries with lower democracy scores generally show higher poverty rates, while those with higher scores cluster near the bottom of the chart. Burundi, Ethiopia, and Benin sit in the upper-left quadrant with democracy indices below 0.4 and poverty rates exceeding 80%. In contrast, Australia, Chile, and Costa Rica appear in the lower-right, combining democracy scores above 0.8 with poverty rates below 20%. Countries like China and Vietnam occupy the lower-left, showing low poverty despite moderate democracy scores, while nations such as Peru and Colombia fall in the middle range on both axes. The distribution shows a clear concentration of high-poverty, low-democracy nations in Africa and parts of Asia, and low-poverty, high-democracy nations in Europe and Oceania.
